facts.text: St. Augustine was founded on September 8, 1565 by Spanish admiral Pedro Menéndez de Avilés, becoming the oldest continuously inhabited European‑settled city in the continental U.S.
Menéndez named the settlement San Agustín after the feast day of St. Augustine, which he first sighted on August 28, 1565.
The Castillo de San Marcos, built in the 17th century, is the oldest masonry fort in the U.S. and later became Fort Marion during the American period.
During the 20‑year British period, St. Augustine remained a strategic naval hub, with Britain renaming the fort to Fort St. Michaels.
St. Augustine’s narrow streets and balconied houses reflect the original town plan laid out by Governor Gonzalo Méndez de Canzo in the late sixteenth century.
The city’s historic district includes George Street, a pedestrian thoroughfare lined with over 30 historic houses that attract visitors year‑round.
